docs.text: Imut til Basalo dala! — Welcome to the Basalo Guide! This site serves as full documentation for Basalo, an international auxiliary language. Don't know what the heck an international auxiliary language is? Wanna know why Basalo exists? Check out About for more information. Want to learn the language? See the Guide and join the Discord ! Need a refresher? See our Reference and feel free to check out the Dictionary !
docs.title: Home

docs.text: This site serves as full documentation for Basalo, an international auxiliary language. Don't know what the heck an international auxiliary language is? Wanna know why Basalo exists? Check out About for more information. Want to learn the language? See the Guide and join the Discord ! Need a refresher? See our Reference and feel free to check out the Dictionary !
docs.title: Imut til Basalo dala! —Welcome to the Basalo Guide!

docs.text: What is Basalo? Basalo is an international auxiliary language: a constructed language that aims to help people all across the world communicate with each other. These languages are meant to be as easy to learn as possible, to help people connect to one another through a shared language. The ethos of Basalo is to be just like a natural language—with none of the irregularity. With a vocabulary from all around the world and easy-to-understand grammar, Basalo aims to be as natural to speak as possible while having none of the difficulties of actual natural languages. Currently, Basalo has reached a stable "Alpha" state, where the grammar, syntax, and derivation methods have been set in stone. See " Development " for more information. The making of Basalo What it used to be Basalo first started out as an IAL largely inspired by Esperanto—in fact, it used the same part of speech ending-vowel-rule, though it substituted -i with -u. In fact, this is why the name "Basalo" has an ending -o; the name is a portmanteau of baso (now basa ) + saramo (now salam ). I moved away from this system in favor of a structure that had no part-of-speech specification, instead allowing (almost) all words to act as verbs, nouns, and/or adjectives. Alapi e Basalo — The Basalo Discord There is an official Discord server where people can discuss and learn Basalo. This place also serves as a public forum for suggesting changes to Basalo now that the language has reached a stable state. Any people who are interested, even if they don't plan to learn Basalo, are welcome to join! Development Any constructed language will be ever-growing and ever-changing, but as it is right now, Basalo is ready to speak! The basis of Basalo is ready for effective communication. If there's no word for something, then anyone is free to request or recommend a word!
docs.title: About

docs.text: Work in progress! Phonology — Sounds The inventory of Basalo is as follows: Labial Coronal Back Plosive (-v) p t k Plosive (+v) b d g Fricative f s, tʃ Approximant v l j Nasal m n Front Central Back High i u Mid e o Low a

docs.text: .entry { border-top: 1px solid #ccc; padding-top: 0.5em; padding-bottom: 0.5em; } .entry-head { padding-left: 1.5em; text-indent: -1.5em; } .entry-details-table { width: 100%; table-layout: fixed; border: solid 0px; padding-left: 1.5em; } .entry-details { color: #000; font-size: 100%; padding-left: 0.5em; border-left: 1px solid #ccc; } Dictionary Here, you can see all of Basalo's vocabulary in one central place! You can search a Basalo or English word in the input box, or search by a specific field by using @field term . This input uses RegEx, so you can use things like ^$ for empty strings or . for any text. The fields are always all-lowercase, and include xx_def , xx_notes , xx_tags (replace xx with a language code, e.g. en for English), etymology , etym_tags , and derive . Click on an entry to expand it. If you don't like looking at all the empty fields, you can select "All non-empty" in the dropdown next to the input box. Show: All All non-empty Go

docs.text: Guide So, you want to learn a language to be able to speak to people all across the world. You've come to the right place! These are simple lessons designed for the layman, so no linguistics jargon here. Looking for a quick look-up on grammar, or wanna see the technical details? See our reference page. 1. Sounds and spelling Learn how to read and write Basalo. Here, we'll take a look at the letters of Basalo and how to pronounce them. 2. How to make a sentence Learn how to make basic sentences. We'll learn the usage of yo , e , and la , how to form statements and questions, and introduce a few core words to play around with. 3. Making new words Learn how to create new words using compounds and kesekmolas . We'll create words for tools and occupations, make causing and becoming words, and learn the rules for compound words. 4. Incomplete, complete, and future actions Learn how to talk about when actions happen. We'll take another look at yo , introduce the particles at and sa , and learn how English tenses match to Basalo's. 5. Understanding doskamolas Learn how to use doskamolas , a collection of very useful words. Here we'll learn how to talk about "this", "every", "none", and "some", and how to ask questions like "how much?" and "who?". 6. How to make a sentence, but harder Learn how to make more complex sentences. We'll learn about modal verbs, noun phrases, and introduce prepositional verbs. 7. Names of people and places Learn how to fit names into Basalo's sound system. We'll learn some Basalo words that come from English, what sounds become which letters, and finish up by reading a few conversations. 8. Introduce yourself Make an introduction in Basalo. This'll include your name, where you're from, what you speak, and your hobbies. Congratulations on making it this far!
docs.title: Guide Index

docs.text: table { max-width: 66%; font-size: 100%; } Lesson 1 — Sounds and spelling Basalo's alphabet consists of 19 letters; 14 consonants and 5 vowels: p, t, k, b, d, g, f, s, c, v, l, y, m, n a, i, u, e, o Sounds The unique thing about Basalo is that its sounds are able to be pronounced a variety of ways. Most are pronounced the same as in English: p, t, k — like in p ay, t ake, k ing b, d, g — like in b ay, d arn, g ame f — like in f amous m, n — like in m orning, n oon y — like in y ear, y awn a — like in f a ther i — like in t ee th or b i t u — like in t oo th or f oo t e — like in b e t o — like in oh Never pronounce vowels as an 'uh' sound, like in b u t ! A couple are pronounced sometimes like in English, but with some variations: s — like in s at, but also like th ink, th at, and z ap v — like in v ine, but also like w ine l — like in l ake, but also like r ake And the last one is pronounced much differently than in English: c — like in ch urch or j ump Stress Basalo has no contrasting stress; there's no difference between words like per mit versus per mit , like in English. Generally, any syllable can be stressed. Examples We'll look at a few words in Basalo and how to pronounce them: Basalo Meaning Pronunciation yati speak YAH-tee kitap book KEE-tahp salam person SAH-lahm, SAH-rahm un one OON ceba sit CHEH-bah, JEH-bah neya thanks! NEH-yah din place DEEN fas make FAHS, FAHZ ven warm WEHN, VEHN taga day TAH-gah Capitalization and punctuation In Basalo, the beginnings of sentences are capitalized, as well as names of people and places (especially if preceded by o ). Beyond that, Basalo uses other punctuation, like question marks, exclamation points, periods, and commas. Imut sin! — "Good to see you!" Sin e mea amal, o Jon! — "Meet my friend, John!" Ka me kan e lel? — "Can I have it?" Me kisa, ga lel yo u. — "I think that he's there." (note: the comma here is optional)
docs.title: Lesson 1 — Pronunciation

docs.text: In Basalo, the beginnings of sentences are capitalized, as well as names of people and places (especially if preceded by o ). Beyond that, Basalo uses other punctuation, like question marks, exclamation points, periods, and commas. Imut sin! — "Good to see you!" Sin e mea amal, o Jon! — "Meet my friend, John!" Ka me kan e lel? — "Can I have it?" Me kisa, ga lel yo u. — "I think that he's there." (note: the comma here is optional)
docs.title: Capitalization and punctuation

docs.text: table { max-width: 80%; font-size: 100%; } Lesson 2 — How to make a sentence Basalo sentences can be put together in a variety of ways, with a few rules: The one doing an action, or the subject , always goes first, The one experiencing an action, or the object , always has a preposition before it, And a noun that's modified by an adjective will always have la before it. For now, the object preposition will always be e , but later on we'll go over verbs with other object prepositions. Never ever forget your preposition! Statements With these rules, the basic structure is as follows: Type Example English do-er, action, object me yo yati e Basalo I'm speaking Basalo do-er, object, action le e umal yo esa he's eating an apple la noun adjective la taga imut a good day adjective la noun naya la yok a new game You'll notice that the verbs in these sentences have yo before them. This word describes an action that's unfinished or still happening, and always goes directly before the verb. However, you can get rid of this word if the do-er is a pronoun: me (yo) sin e salam. — I see a person. les e me (yo) dop ka? — Can they help me? mes e kafe (yo) esa! — We're drinking some coffee! Questions Questions are made by putting the question particle ka at the beginning or end of a sentence; sort of like a spoken question mark. le yo kan e kitap. — He has a book. ka le yo kan e kitap? — Does he have a book? le yo kan e kitap ka? — Does he have a book? Negatives In general, you can make a sentence negative by putting ma before the yo (or, as we'll see later on, the at or sa ): aman yo nalu. — The water ripples. aman ma yo nalu. — The water is not rippling. You can negate a specific word in a sentence by putting ma before it. ma inaki yo esa e mis. — The dog isn't eating the meat. inaki yo ma esa e mis. — The dog isn't eating the meat. inaki yo esa e ma mis. — The dog isn't eating the meat . Wait, how do you say "the" and "a"? You don't! Or, more precisely, there's no dedicated word for either of these. If you want to refer to one thing, you could use un , meaning "one": mama — the parent, a parent un mama — a parent, one parent And if you want to refer to some specific object, you could use lel , which means "this" or "that". We'll become more acquainted with words related to lel in Lesson 5, as it's one of the doskamolas : kin — the plant, a plant lel kin — that specific plant Remember, don't get confused with the word la ; it's just there to mark what word is a noun: la oto naya or naya la oto — a new car, the new car la umal idaman or idaman la umal — a red apple, the red apple Using lel versus le Both lel and le are very versatile words which we'll see a lot in the coming lessons. In short, le means "he" or "she", while lel can mean "this" or "that". le yo meti e lel kitap. — He's putting down that book. tes sin e le ka? — Do you guys see her? When we use lel before a noun earlier in a sentence, we can refer to that whole noun by just using lel , like so: lel salam, ka lel yo kan e mea kitap? — That guy , does he have my book? te yo sin e lel naja la yok? lel yo e aga kul. — Are you seeing that new game ? It 's super cool. Some words to start with Now that we know how to put sentences together, here's a few words you can play around with: me , te , le — I/me, you, he/she mes , tes , les — we/us, you all, they lel — this, that salam — person, man, woman mama — parent, mother, father kitap — to read; book sin — to see, look at esa — to eat, to drink; food, beverage yati — to speak, talk; speech Don't forget the important sentence-building words yo , e and la !
docs.title: Lesson 2 — Sentences

For now, the object preposition will always be e , but later on we'll go over verbs with other object prepositions. Never ever forget your preposition!

docs.text: table { max-width: 100%; font-size: 100%; } Work in progress! Lesson 3 — Making new words Basalo's vocabulary has many terms made up of multiple words. While sometimes these compound words are made of two full words, most of the time they're made up of one full word and one or more affixes, which in Basalo are called kesekumolas . There's quite a bit, but don't worry! We won't need to learn them all now, and you'll come to recognize them in words with time. For now, let's look at some of the most useful ones: kesekumol Meaning Examples -(a)lo tool, item naso "to write" → nasolo "pencil, pen" -(a)pi becoming, changing al "knowledge" → alapi "to learn" -(a)tel do-er, occupation fas "to create" → fasatel "creator" -(i)gi causing, result iso "equal" → isogi "to balance" -(n)ik little, small siku "ice" → sikunik "snow"
docs.title: Lesson 3 — Derivation
